impossibility_statement: The Price equation (1970) decomposes evolutionary change into selection and transmission components: Delta(z_bar) = Cov(w,z)/w_bar + E(w*delta_z)/w_bar. A population cannot simultaneously maximize mean fitness, maintain genetic variance, and ensure faithful transmission. Selection that increases mean trait value necessarily depletes the covariance term (Fisher's fundamental theorem), while maintaining variance requires mutation/recombination that corrupts the transmission term.
formal_source: Price, G.R. (1970) 'Selection and Covariance', Nature 227:520-521; Frank, S.A. (1997) 'The Price Equation, Fisher's Fundamental Theorem, Kin Selection, and Causal Analysis', Evolution 51(6):1712-1729
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(selection + transmission) -> COMPLETE(simultaneous optimization) FAILS -> BREAK_SYMMETRY(sacrifice one component)
why_closure_fails: The covariance between fitness and trait value is consumed by selection (variance depletion), while maintaining variance requires imperfect transmission. The two terms of the Price equation are mathematically coupled through the fitness-variance relationship: Var(w) > 0 implies eventual fixation under pure selection.

impossibility_statement: Fisher's fundamental theorem (1930): the rate of increase in mean fitness equals the additive genetic variance in fitness divided by mean fitness. This is an exact theorem, but it only accounts for the partial change due to natural selection. The total change in mean fitness can decrease because environmental deterioration and frequency-dependent effects can overwhelm the selection component. A population cannot simultaneously have positive additive variance (evolvability) and stable mean fitness under environmental change.
formal_source: Fisher, R.A. (1930) 'The Genetical Theory of Natural Selection'; Price (1972) exact proof; Ewens (1989) modern interpretation
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(additive selection + environmental change) -> COMPLETE(net fitness increase) FAILS -> BREAK_SYMMETRY(sacrifice adaptability or stability)
why_closure_fails: The partial change dw_bar/dt = V_A(w)/w_bar >= 0 is always non-negative, but total fitness change includes environmental deterioration terms that can be arbitrarily negative. The Red Queen effect: adaptation to a moving target consumes variance without net progress.

impossibility_statement: In Kauffman's NK model, a genome of N loci with K epistatic interactions per locus cannot simultaneously have: smooth fitness landscapes (enabling gradient-following evolution), high epistatic complexity (K close to N), and polynomial-time reachability of global optima. When K=0 the landscape is smooth with one peak (Mt. Fuji) but has no epistatic richness. When K=N-1 the landscape is maximally rugged (uncorrelated random field) and finding the global optimum is NP-hard.
formal_source: Kauffman & Levin (1987); Kauffman (1993) 'The Origins of Order'; Weinberger (1991) proved autocorrelation length = 1/(K+1); Wright et al. (2000) NP-hardness of NK optimization for K >= 2
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(epistasis + landscape navigation) -> COMPLETE(efficient global optimization) FAILS -> BREAK_SYMMETRY(reduce K or accept local optima)
why_closure_fails: Weinberger (1991) showed landscape autocorrelation r(d) = (1-d/N)^(K+1) for Hamming distance d. As K increases, autocorrelation drops exponentially, destroying gradient information. For K >= 2, Wright et al. proved finding global optima is NP-hard by reduction from MAX-SAT.

impossibility_statement: In a Lotka-Volterra system with n species competing for fewer than n limiting resources at equilibrium, coexistence is impossible. If n species compete for k < n resources, at most k species survive at stable equilibrium. Formally: for the system dN_i/dt = N_i(r_i - sum_j(a_ij * N_j)), stable coexistence of n species requires n independent limiting factors.
formal_source: Gause (1934) experimental; Levin (1970) rigorous proof via Lyapunov analysis; Armstrong & McGehee (1980) extended to nonequilibrium; MacArthur (1970) resource-consumer proof
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(n species + k<n resources) -> COMPLETE(stable coexistence) FAILS -> BREAK_SYMMETRY(niche differentiation or nonequilibrium dynamics)
why_closure_fails: The competition matrix A must be invertible for n-species coexistence, requiring rank(A) >= n. With k < n resources, the consumption matrix C (n x k) has rank <= k < n, so A = C*diag(resource_supply)*C^T is rank-deficient. The system has fewer independent equations than unknowns, forcing competitive exclusion.

impossibility_statement: For computationally universal cellular automata (e.g., Rule 110, Game of Life), there exists no general shortcut to predict the state at time t without simulating all t steps. Formally: predicting the t-step evolution of a universal CA is P-complete (Neary & Woods 2006 for Rule 110), meaning any polynomial speedup would collapse P. Moreover, specific decision problems about CA long-term behavior (halting, periodicity) are undecidable by reduction from the halting problem.
formal_source: Wolfram (1985) conjecture; Cook (2004) proof Rule 110 is Turing-complete; Neary & Woods (2006) P-completeness; Kari (1990) undecidability of reversibility for 2D CA
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(local rules + universality) -> COMPLETE(efficient prediction) FAILS -> BREAK_SYMMETRY(restrict rule class or accept simulation cost)
why_closure_fails: Turing completeness of the CA means it can simulate any computation. A general prediction shortcut would solve the halting problem (predict whether a simulated TM halts). P-completeness means the t-step prediction is as hard as any problem in P, ruling out NC (highly parallel) shortcuts unless NC = P.

impossibility_statement: Shannon's rate-distortion theorem applied to neural coding: a neural channel with capacity C bits/sec cannot represent a source with entropy rate H at distortion level D if the rate-distortion function R(D) > C. Specifically, for a neuron with firing rate f_max and refractory period tau, channel capacity C <= f_max * log2(1/f_max*tau) bits/sec. No coding scheme (rate coding, temporal coding, population coding with N neurons) can exceed N*C bits/sec total, forcing a tradeoff between temporal precision, stimulus range, and coding reliability.
formal_source: Shannon (1959) rate-distortion theory; Rieke et al. (1997) 'Spikes'; Borst & Theunissen (1999) information-theoretic neural coding limits
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(channel capacity + source entropy) -> COMPLETE(lossless representation) FAILS -> BREAK_SYMMETRY(sacrifice precision, range, or add neurons)
why_closure_fails: Rate-distortion function R(D) is convex decreasing: achieving distortion D requires rate >= R(D). Neural channel capacity is bounded by biophysical constraints (refractory period, metabolic cost). The gap R(D) - C > 0 is irreducible for a given D and neural resource budget, proven by Shannon's converse theorem.

impossibility_statement: In Valiant's (2009) formal model of evolvability, evolution is modeled as a restricted form of statistical query learning. A concept class is evolvable iff it is learnable by statistical queries with tolerance. Boolean functions requiring superpolynomial statistical queries (e.g., parities, cryptographic functions) are provably not evolvable in polynomial generations. Evolution cannot discover arbitrary Boolean functions; it is strictly weaker than PAC learning.
formal_source: Valiant, L. (2009) 'Evolvability', J. ACM 56(1):3; Feldman (2008) proved evolvability equivalent to correlation-based SQ learning
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(mutation + selection + polynomial time) -> COMPLETE(arbitrary function learning) FAILS -> BREAK_SYMMETRY(restrict target class or allow exponential time)
why_closure_fails: Parity functions over n bits require 2^(n/2) statistical queries with any tolerance (Blum et al. 1994 SQ lower bound). Since evolution in Valiant's model is restricted to SQ learning, it provably cannot evolve parity-like functions in polynomial generations. The mutation operator's locality prevents discovery of functions with high-order correlations.

impossibility_statement: Kashtan & Alon (2005) showed modular network architectures evolve only under modularly varying goals. Under a fixed goal, evolution produces non-modular but higher-performing solutions. Formally: for a fixed Boolean function f, the minimum circuit size C(f) <= C_modular(f), with strict inequality for most functions. A population cannot simultaneously achieve globally optimal performance and maintain modular architecture unless the fitness landscape itself has modular structure.
formal_source: Kashtan & Alon (2005) PNAS; Clune et al. (2013) formalized connection costs; Lipson (2007) constructive proof via circuit complexity arguments
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(optimization + modularity + adaptability) -> COMPLETE(all three) FAILS -> BREAK_SYMMETRY(sacrifice optimality or modularity)
why_closure_fails: For a random Boolean function on n inputs, Shannon (1949) showed almost all functions require circuits of size Theta(2^n/n). Modular circuits restrict to compositions of smaller functions, spanning only a measure-zero subset of all functions. The modular decomposition imposes structural constraints that prevent reaching the globally optimal non-modular circuit for most fitness landscapes.
key_references: Clune et al 2013 Proc R Soc B

impossibility_statement: For a diploid population of effective size N_e, a mutation with selective coefficient |s| << 1/(2*N_e) is effectively invisible to selection and governed entirely by drift. Formally, the fixation probability u(s) = (1 - e^(-4*N_e*s*p)) / (1 - e^(-4*N_e*s)) (Kimura 1962). When |4*N_e*s| < 1, selection cannot distinguish beneficial from neutral alleles. A finite population cannot simultaneously maintain selection efficiency on weak-effect alleles and avoid drift-dominated dynamics.
formal_source: Kimura (1962) fixation probability formula (exact diffusion solution); Kimura (1968) neutral theory; Ohta (1973) nearly neutral theory
desired_properties: efficient selection on all beneficial mutations
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(selection + finite population) -> COMPLETE(deterministic allele fate) FAILS -> BREAK_SYMMETRY(increase N_e or accept drift dominance)
why_closure_fails: The diffusion equation for allele frequency has a stochastic term proportional to 1/sqrt(N_e). When the deterministic selection force s is of order 1/N_e, the signal-to-noise ratio is O(1), making the allele's fate indeterminate. This is an exact result from the Kolmogorov forward equation, not an approximation.

impossibility_statement: West, Brown & Enquist (1997) derived from first principles that metabolic rate B scales as B ~ M^(3/4) for organisms with fractal-like distribution networks that minimize transport costs while serving all cells. An organism cannot simultaneously: have a space-filling distribution network, minimize total hydrodynamic resistance, and have metabolic rate scale linearly with mass. The 3/4 exponent is a mathematical consequence of network geometry optimization in 3D.
formal_source: West, Brown & Enquist (1997) Science 276:122-126; Banavar et al. (1999) independent derivation via network optimization; Savage et al. (2004) empirical validation across 27 orders of magnitude
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(space-filling + cost-minimization + 3D embedding) -> COMPLETE(linear scaling) FAILS -> BREAK_SYMMETRY(accept sublinear scaling or non-fractal network)
why_closure_fails: In a self-similar branching network filling a 3D volume, the number of terminal units scales as M but the network's total resistance imposes a bottleneck. The fractal branching optimization yields the scaling exponent d/(d+1) where d=3 is the embedding dimension, giving 3/4. This follows from the constraint that terminal units must tile the volume while minimizing dissipation. NOTE: The exact 3/4 exponent is debated; some argue for 2/3 from surface-area arguments. The impossibility of linear scaling under network constraints is robust.

impossibility_statement: The Information Bottleneck method (Tishby et al. 1999) shows that any compressed representation T of input X that preserves information about target Y is bounded by the Markov chain X -> T -> Y. The data processing inequality gives I(T;Y) <= I(X;Y) and I(T;Y) <= I(T;X). For a given compression level I(T;X) = R, the maximum achievable relevance I(T;Y) is bounded by the IB curve, which is the Pareto frontier. No representation can be above this curve. This is a proven variational bound.
formal_source: Tishby, Pereira & Bialek (1999) 'The Information Bottleneck Method'; Gilad-Bachrach et al. (2003) proved IB curve properties; Shwartz-Ziv & Tishby (2017) deep learning interpretation
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(compression + relevance preservation) -> COMPLETE(both maximal) FAILS -> BREAK_SYMMETRY(trade compression for relevance along IB curve)
why_closure_fails: The data processing inequality (DPI) is an exact consequence of the Markov chain condition. For deterministic maps, the IB curve is further constrained. The Lagrangian L = I(T;X) - beta*I(T;Y) yields a family of Blahut-Arimoto-type self-consistent equations whose solutions trace the optimal frontier. Points above the frontier violate DPI.

impossibility_statement: For any stable, minimum-phase, causal LTI system, the phase at any frequency omega_0 is uniquely determined by the gain curve via the Hilbert transform: angle(G(j*omega_0)) = (1/pi) * PV integral of (d(ln|G|)/d(ln omega)) * ln|coth(|u|/2)| du, where u = ln(omega/omega_0). A designer cannot independently specify gain and phase responses. Changing gain slope at any frequency forces phase changes everywhere.
formal_source: Bode, H.W. (1945) 'Network Analysis and Feedback Amplifier Design'; the relation follows from the Hilbert transform (Kramers-Kronig) applied to the log of the transfer function
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(gain + phase + minimum-phase) -> COMPLETE(independent specification) FAILS -> BREAK_SYMMETRY(allow non-minimum-phase zeros or sacrifice gain/phase freedom)
why_closure_fails: For a minimum-phase system, ln(G(s)) is analytic in the right half-plane. By Cauchy's integral formula, the real part (log-gain) and imaginary part (phase) form a Hilbert transform pair. This is an exact mathematical identity from complex analysis, not an approximation. Each -20 dB/decade slope forces -90 degrees of phase.

impossibility_statement: The Kalman filter achieves the minimum mean-square estimation error for linear Gaussian systems. For any other estimator E applied to y = Cx + v (Gaussian noise), MSE(E) >= MSE(Kalman) = tr(P), where P satisfies the Riccati equation P = APA' - APC'(CPC'+R)^(-1)CPA' + Q. No estimator can beat the Kalman filter for linear Gaussian systems, and the steady-state error P_ss > 0 unless the system is fully observable and noise-free. This establishes a floor on estimation accuracy.
formal_source: Kalman (1960) exact optimality proof; Anderson & Moore (1979) Riccati equation convergence; the bound is a special case of Cramer-Rao for Gaussian models
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(noisy observations + linear dynamics) -> COMPLETE(perfect state estimation) FAILS -> BREAK_SYMMETRY(accept Riccati-bounded error or add sensors)
why_closure_fails: The Riccati equation P = APA' + Q - APC'(CPC'+R)^(-1)CPA' has P > 0 whenever Q > 0 (process noise exists). The positive-definiteness of P_ss is guaranteed by the stabilizability/detectability conditions. This is the Bayesian Cramer-Rao bound specialized to the Gaussian linear case: the Fisher information matrix determines the irreducible estimation error.

impossibility_statement: The Small Gain Theorem: a feedback interconnection of two stable systems G and Delta is stable for ALL perturbations ||Delta||_inf <= 1/gamma iff ||G||_inf < gamma. A system with high loop gain (||G||_inf >= gamma) for performance CANNOT be robustly stable against all perturbations of size 1/gamma. The H-infinity norm of the nominal system directly limits the size of tolerable perturbations.
formal_source: Zames (1966) original small gain theorem; Doyle (1982) structured singular value generalization; Zhou, Doyle & Glover (1996) modern treatment
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(performance gain + robustness margin) -> COMPLETE(both maximal) FAILS -> BREAK_SYMMETRY(sacrifice performance or robustness)
why_closure_fails: ||G||_inf * ||Delta||_inf < 1 is necessary and sufficient for stability of the feedback loop (for unstructured perturbations). This is a direct consequence of the Nyquist stability criterion generalized to operator norms. The product of gain norms is a conserved quantity: increasing one factor forces decreasing the other.

hub_name: Strong Converse to Channel Coding Theorem
impossibility_statement: For a discrete memoryless channel with capacity C, any code with rate R > C has error probability P_e -> 1 exponentially fast as block length n -> infinity. Specifically, P_e >= 1 - 2^(-n*E_sp(R,C)) where E_sp is the sphere-packing exponent. The strong converse (Wolfowitz 1957, Arimoto 1973) proves this is not merely that reliable communication fails, but that communication becomes catastrophically unreliable: the error probability approaches 1, not just stays bounded away from 0.
formal_source: Shannon (1948) weak converse; Wolfowitz (1957) strong converse for DMC; Arimoto (1973) exponential strong converse; Polyanskiy, Poor & Verdu (2010) finite blocklength converse
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(rate above capacity + reliable decoding) -> COMPLETE(low error communication) FAILS -> BREAK_SYMMETRY(reduce rate or accept exponential error growth)
why_closure_fails: The sphere-packing bound: the number of distinguishable codewords in n dimensions is bounded by the volume of the output space divided by the volume of noise-typical sets. For R > C, codeword spheres must overlap, and the overlap fraction grows exponentially. This is a combinatorial packing argument with information-theoretic tightness.

impossibility_statement: For a feedback system with plant P(s) having p unstable poles {p_i} and z unstable zeros {z_j}, the weighted sensitivity integral satisfies: integral_0^inf ln|S(j*omega)| * W(omega) d_omega = pi * sum_i Re(p_i) + corrections from unstable zeros. When unstable zeros are present, the waterbed is amplified: sensitivity reduction in one band causes GREATER-than-equal increase elsewhere. The penalty grows with the proximity of unstable poles and zeros.
formal_source: Bode (1945) original integral; Freudenberg & Looze (1985) generalization to NMP systems; Seron, Braslavsky & Goodwin (1997) MIMO generalization
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(sensitivity reduction + unstable poles/zeros) -> COMPLETE(global sensitivity reduction) FAILS -> BREAK_SYMMETRY(accept amplified waterbed or redesign plant)
why_closure_fails: For a plant with RHP zero z and RHP pole p, the Poisson integral constraint adds a term 2*Re(p)*Re(z)/|p+conj(z)|^2 to the sensitivity integral. When p and z are close, this term dominates, forcing extreme sensitivity peaking. The proof uses Blaschke products and the Poisson-Jensen formula from complex analysis.

impossibility_statement: In MIMO (multi-input multi-output) systems, the sensitivity function S(s) = (I + G(s)K(s))^(-1) satisfies: for any plant G with RHP zero z (output direction u_z, input direction v_z), the sensitivity in direction u_z at frequency omega = Im(z) cannot be made less than |u_z' * S(j*omega) * u_z| >= product over RHP poles p_i of |z - p_i|/|z + conj(p_i)|. The plant's directional structure imposes irreducible constraints: you cannot independently shape sensitivity in different I/O directions.
formal_source: Chen (2000) interpolation constraints; Skogestad & Postlethwaite (2005) MIMO limitations; Havre & Skogestad (2003) directional sensitivity bounds
desired_properties: independent sensitivity shaping per I/O channel
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(multivariable control + directional performance) -> COMPLETE(independent per-channel optimization) FAILS -> BREAK_SYMMETRY(accept directional coupling or decouple plant)
why_closure_fails: The MIMO interpolation conditions require S(z) * u_z = u_z for each RHP zero z, meaning sensitivity must be exactly 1 in the zero direction at the zero frequency. This is a hard algebraic constraint from the plant's Smith-McMillan structure, proven via Nevanlinna-Pick interpolation theory. The MIMO waterbed is direction-dependent and generally more severe than SISO.

impossibility_statement: Pontryagin's Maximum Principle (1956): for a control system dx/dt = f(x,u) minimizing J = integral L(x,u)dt + phi(x(T)), the optimal control must satisfy the Hamiltonian condition H(x*,u*,lambda) >= H(x*,u,lambda) for all admissible u, where lambda satisfies the costate equation d_lambda/dt = -dH/dx. When the control constraint set U is bounded, the optimal trajectory generically has BANG-BANG structure (switching between extremes). No smooth optimal control exists for most constrained problems. The costate equation creates a two-point boundary value problem that is generically ill-conditioned.
formal_source: Pontryagin et al. (1962) 'Mathematical Theory of Optimal Processes'; Bryson & Ho (1975); the bang-bang theorem is proven for linear systems (LaSalle 1960)
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(bounded control + optimality + smoothness) -> COMPLETE(smooth optimal trajectory) FAILS -> BREAK_SYMMETRY(accept bang-bang switching or relax bounds)
why_closure_fails: For linear systems, the switching function sigma(t) = lambda'(t)*B determines control direction. When sigma crosses zero, control must switch between extremes (LaSalle's bang-bang theorem). The set of smooth optimal controls has measure zero in the space of all optimal controls for bounded-input systems. The costate BVP has exponentially growing modes that make numerical solution ill-conditioned (the 'curse of sensitivity').

impossibility_statement: For a SISO feedback system, the complementary sensitivity T(s) = 1 - S(s) satisfies its own integral constraint: for plants with time delay tau, integral_0^inf (1/omega^2) * ln|T(j*omega)| d_omega <= pi*tau/2. Tracking (requiring T close to 1) and noise rejection (requiring T close to 0) at high frequencies cannot both be achieved. The time delay tau sets an absolute limit: no controller can make the closed-loop bandwidth exceed approximately 1/tau while maintaining stability margins.
formal_source: Middleton (1991) tracking limitations; Seron, Braslavsky & Goodwin (1997) fundamental limitations textbook; Sung & Hara (1988) complementary sensitivity integral
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(tracking + noise rejection + time delay) -> COMPLETE(both perfect) FAILS -> BREAK_SYMMETRY(limit bandwidth or accept noise amplification)
why_closure_fails: S + T = I is an exact algebraic identity. The Bode integral for S and the complementary integral for T are dual constraints. With time delay e^(-tau*s), the phase loss of tau*omega radians forces T to roll off before omega = 1/tau, proven via the Poisson integral formula applied to the delay term. The delay creates an irreducible conflict between tracking speed and noise sensitivity.

impossibility_statement: Zames (1981) formulated the H-infinity optimal control problem: minimize ||W*S||_inf over all stabilizing controllers, where W is a performance weighting and S is the sensitivity function. The minimum achievable value gamma_opt > 0 is determined by the plant's unstable poles and zeros via Nevanlinna-Pick interpolation. For a plant with RHP pole p and RHP zero z, gamma_opt >= |p + conj(z)|/|p - z|. No controller can reduce weighted sensitivity below this plant-intrinsic bound.
formal_source: Zames (1981) 'Feedback and optimal sensitivity'; Francis & Zames (1984) H-infinity solution; Doyle et al. (1989) state-space solution
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(sensitivity minimization + stability + unstable plant) -> COMPLETE(arbitrarily low sensitivity) FAILS -> BREAK_SYMMETRY(accept gamma_opt floor or redesign plant)
why_closure_fails: The Nevanlinna-Pick interpolation condition requires S(p_i) = 1 for each RHP pole p_i (for internal stability) and S(z_j) = 0 for ideal disturbance rejection at zeros. These interpolation constraints, combined with the H-infinity norm constraint, create an irreducible lower bound. The proof uses the Sarason generalization of the Nehari theorem in functional analysis.

impossibility_statement: May (1972) proved that for a random community matrix A (n species, connectance C, interaction strength sigma), the system is almost surely unstable when sigma*sqrt(n*C) > 1. A large, richly connected ecosystem cannot simultaneously be: species-rich (large n), strongly interacting (large sigma), densely connected (large C), and locally stable. The probability of stability drops to zero exponentially as n*C*sigma^2 increases past 1.
formal_source: May (1972) Nature 238:413-414; proof via Wigner's semicircle law for random matrices; Allesina & Tang (2012) refined bounds for structured interaction types
structural_pattern: COMPOSE(diversity + interaction strength + connectivity) -> COMPLETE(stable equilibrium) FAILS -> BREAK_SYMMETRY(reduce one factor or accept instability)
why_closure_fails: The eigenvalue distribution of a random n x n matrix with i.i.d. entries of variance sigma^2 follows Girko's circular law: eigenvalues fill a disk of radius sigma*sqrt(n). For the community matrix -I + A, stability requires all eigenvalues to have negative real parts, which fails when the spectral radius sigma*sqrt(n*C) > 1. This is an exact result from random matrix theory.
